content.explanation:
Every object in JavaScript has a hidden internal property called [[Prototype]] that points to another object — its prototype. This is the foundation of JavaScript's inheritance model, which is fundamentally different from class-based languages like Java or C++. When you access a property on an object, JavaScript first looks at the object itself. If the property is not found, the engine walks up the prototype chain — checking the object's prototype, then that prototype's prototype, and so on — until it either finds the property or reaches an object whose prototype is null (typically Object.prototype, whose own [[Prototype]] is null).

There is an important distinction between __proto__ and the prototype property. The __proto__ property (now standardized as Object.getPrototypeOf()) is a getter/setter that exists on every object and points to the object's actual prototype — the object it inherits from. The prototype property, on the other hand, only exists on functions. When a function is used as a constructor with the new keyword, the newly created object's __proto__ is set to the constructor function's prototype property. This is how methods defined on Constructor.prototype become available to all instances.

Object.create() is the most direct way to set up prototypal inheritance. It creates a new object with its [[Prototype]] set to the object you pass as the first argument. This gives you fine-grained control without involving constructor functions or the new keyword. It is frequently used when you want clean delegation chains or when implementing inheritance between constructor functions.

Constructor functions were the original pattern for creating objects with shared behavior in JavaScript. When you call a function with new, four things happen: a new empty object is created, its __proto__ is linked to the constructor's prototype, the constructor runs with this bound to the new object, and the object is returned (unless the constructor explicitly returns a different object). All instances created by the same constructor share methods defined on the constructor's prototype, which is memory-efficient compared to defining methods inside the constructor.

The hasOwnProperty method and the in operator behave differently when it comes to inherited properties. hasOwnProperty returns true only if the property exists directly on the object, ignoring the prototype chain. The in operator returns true if the property exists anywhere in the prototype chain. Understanding this distinction is critical when iterating over object properties — for...in loops walk the entire chain, which is why you often see hasOwnProperty checks inside them.

Performance-wise, keeping prototype chains short is generally better. Deep chains mean more lookups when resolving properties. In practice, most chains are only two or three levels deep (instance -> Constructor.prototype -> Object.prototype), so this rarely becomes a bottleneck. However, relying heavily on prototype chain lookups for frequently accessed properties in hot code paths can have a measurable impact. Caching looked-up values in local variables or using direct property assignment are common optimizations.

codeExamples.title: Prototype chain and property lookup
codeExamples.output:
true
true
Animal walks
true
true
true
null
codeExamples.explanation: rabbit does not have 'eats' or 'walk' directly. JavaScript walks up to animal (its prototype) and finds them there. The chain continues to Object.prototype and finally to null.

codeExamples.title: Constructor functions and prototype
codeExamples.output:
Hi, I'm Alice and I'm 25
Hi, I'm Bob and I'm 30
Homo sapiens
true
true
false
true
codeExamples.explanation: Methods on Person.prototype are shared across all instances, saving memory. Instance properties like name and age are unique to each object. hasOwnProperty confirms that greet lives on the prototype, not the instance.

codeExamples.title: Object.create for inheritance between constructors
codeExamples.output:
A red shape
78.54
true
true
true
codeExamples.explanation: Object.create(Shape.prototype) creates a new object whose __proto__ is Shape.prototype. This becomes Circle's prototype, establishing the inheritance chain. Shape.call(this, color) ensures the parent constructor initializes instance properties.

codeExamples.title: hasOwnProperty vs in operator
codeExamples.output:
true
false
true
true
true
false
Own: own
Inherited: inherited
codeExamples.explanation: hasOwnProperty only returns true for properties directly on the object. The in operator checks the entire prototype chain. This distinction matters in for...in loops where you usually want to skip inherited properties.

notes: Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) is the modern, standard way to read an object's prototype. __proto__ is deprecated but still widely supported.
Modifying a built-in prototype (e.g., Array.prototype) is called monkey-patching and is strongly discouraged in production code.
Object.create(null) creates an object with no prototype at all — useful for pure dictionaries without inherited methods like toString.
The prototype chain ends at Object.prototype, whose own [[Prototype]] is null.
commonPitfalls: Confusing __proto__ (the actual prototype link on every object) with the prototype property (which only exists on functions and is used when constructing new instances).
Forgetting to reset the constructor property after using Object.create() to set up inheritance — c.constructor would point to the parent instead of the child.
Using for...in without a hasOwnProperty guard, accidentally iterating over inherited enumerable properties.
Assigning a new object to Constructor.prototype after instances have been created — existing instances still reference the old prototype object.
interviewTips: Be ready to draw or verbally trace the prototype chain for a given object, explaining each link from the instance to null.
Clearly articulate: __proto__ is on every object and points to its prototype; prototype is on functions and becomes the __proto__ of instances created with new.
Explain how property shadowing works: setting a property on an instance creates an own property that shadows the prototype's property without modifying the prototype.
Know that Object.create(null) creates a prototype-less object, and explain when this is useful (e.g., safe hash maps).

exercises.title: Prototype Chain Builder
exercises.description: Build a prototype chain using Object.create. Create a 'vehicle' object with a 'type' property and a describe() method. Create a 'car' that inherits from vehicle, adding its own 'brand' property. Verify the chain by checking hasOwnProperty and the in operator on both own and inherited properties.
exercises.expectedOutput:
A sedan made by Toyota
true
false
true
true
hints: Object.create(vehicle) creates a new object whose [[Prototype]] is vehicle. The new object inherits all of vehicle's properties and methods.
Setting car.type = 'sedan' creates an own property on car that shadows vehicle.type. hasOwnProperty distinguishes own from inherited properties.

exercises.title: Constructor Function with Shared Methods
exercises.description: Create a constructor function 'Book' that sets title and author as instance properties. Add a 'summary' method on the prototype. Create two instances and verify that both share the same prototype method but have their own instance properties.
exercises.expectedOutput:
"1984" by Orwell
"Dune" by Herbert
true
false
true
hints: Methods placed on Constructor.prototype are shared across all instances created with 'new', saving memory compared to defining methods inside the constructor.
hasOwnProperty returns false for 'summary' because it lives on Book.prototype, not on the instance itself.

content.explanation:
ES6 classes, introduced in 2015, provide a cleaner and more familiar syntax for creating objects and managing inheritance in JavaScript. Under the hood, classes are syntactic sugar over JavaScript's existing prototypal inheritance — the class keyword does not introduce a new object model. A class declaration creates a function (the constructor), and methods defined in the class body are placed on that function's prototype property, exactly as you would do manually with constructor functions.

The constructor method is a special method that runs when you create a new instance with the new keyword. Each class can have at most one constructor. Inside it, you initialize instance properties using this. If you do not define a constructor, JavaScript provides a default empty one. For subclasses, the default constructor calls super(...args), forwarding all arguments to the parent constructor.

Static methods and properties belong to the class itself, not to instances. You declare them with the static keyword. They are useful for utility functions that are logically related to the class but do not operate on instance data. For example, a User class might have a static fromJSON(json) method that creates a User from a JSON string. Static methods cannot access this to reference instance data — this inside a static method refers to the class itself.

Private fields and methods, denoted by the # prefix, are a relatively recent addition (ES2022). They are truly private — they cannot be accessed or detected from outside the class body. This is enforced by the JavaScript engine, not by convention. Before private fields existed, developers used the underscore prefix (_property) as a naming convention to signal "do not access directly," but this provided no actual enforcement. Private fields solve this problem definitively.

Getters and setters let you define properties that execute a function when read or written. They are defined using the get and set keywords inside a class body. This is powerful for validation, computed properties, or lazy initialization. From the outside, they look like normal property access — the caller does not know a function is running behind the scenes.

Class expressions are an alternative to class declarations. Just like function expressions, you can assign an anonymous or named class to a variable. Named class expressions have their name scoped to the class body, which can be useful for self-referencing. Class expressions are particularly useful when you need to create classes dynamically or pass them as arguments to functions.

codeExamples.title: Basic class with constructor and methods
codeExamples.output:
2024 Toyota Camry
Toyota Camry is going 60 km/h
Toyota Camry is going 100 km/h
Toyota Camry slowed to 70 km/h
function
codeExamples.explanation: The constructor initializes instance properties. Methods like accelerate and brake are defined on Car.prototype, shared across all instances. toString overrides Object.prototype.toString for a custom string representation.

codeExamples.title: Static methods and properties
codeExamples.explanation: Static members are accessed via the class name, not instances. They serve as namespace-scoped utilities. Attempting to call a static method on an instance returns undefined because the method is on the constructor function, not on its prototype.

codeExamples.title: Private fields and methods
codeExamples.output:
1000
1500
1300
2
Private field '#balance' must be declared in an enclosing class
codeExamples.explanation: Private fields (#balance, #transactionLog) and private methods (#log) cannot be accessed from outside the class body. The getter 'balance' provides controlled read access. The history getter returns a copy to prevent external mutation of the internal log.

codeExamples.title: Getters, setters, and class expressions
codeExamples.explanation: This class expression demonstrates getters and setters for computed and validated properties. Setting fahrenheit internally uses the celsius setter, which enforces the absolute zero constraint. From the caller's perspective, these look like plain properties.

notes: Classes are NOT hoisted like function declarations. A class must be defined before it can be instantiated.
The typeof a class is 'function' — proof that classes are syntactic sugar over constructor functions.
Private fields must be declared in the class body before use. You cannot dynamically add private fields.
All code inside a class body runs in strict mode automatically.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting that classes are not hoisted — using a class before its declaration throws a ReferenceError, unlike function declarations.
Trying to access private fields (#) outside the class body — this is a hard SyntaxError, not just undefined.
Defining a getter without a setter (or vice versa) when both read and write access are needed — writing to a getter-only property silently fails in non-strict mode.
Calling a class without new — unlike constructor functions, classes always throw a TypeError if invoked without new.

exercises.title: Class with Static Factory
exercises.description: Create a 'Player' class with name and score properties, a describe() method, and a static method 'createMVP' that returns a Player with score 100. Demonstrate that the static method is on the class, not on instances.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Alice: 50 pts
Bob: 100 pts
function
undefined
hints: Static methods are defined with the 'static' keyword and called on the class itself (Player.createMVP), not on instances.
Inside a static method, 'this' refers to the class, not an instance. You can use 'new Player(...)' or 'new this(...)' to create instances.

exercises.title: Private Fields with Getter Validation
exercises.description: Create a 'Wallet' class with a private #balance field. Add a deposit method, a getter for balance, and a setter that rejects negative values. Demonstrate that the private field cannot be accessed directly from outside.
hints: Private fields declared with # are only accessible within the class body. The getter provides controlled read access.
The setter throws an error to prevent direct modification, forcing users to go through the deposit method.

content.explanation:
Inheritance in JavaScript classes is established using the extends keyword. When class Child extends Parent, the child class inherits all methods and properties defined on the parent's prototype. Under the hood, two prototype links are set up: Child.prototype.__proto__ points to Parent.prototype (so instances inherit methods), and Child.__proto__ points to Parent (so static methods are inherited too). This dual-link mechanism is what makes JavaScript's class inheritance work seamlessly.

The super keyword serves two purposes. Inside a constructor, super() calls the parent class constructor. This is mandatory in any subclass constructor — you must call super() before using this, or JavaScript will throw a ReferenceError. Outside the constructor, super.method() calls a method on the parent's prototype, which is essential when you override a method but still need the parent's behavior as part of your implementation.

Method overriding is how polymorphism manifests in JavaScript. When a child class defines a method with the same name as one in the parent, the child's version takes precedence. This happens naturally through the prototype chain — since the child's prototype is checked before the parent's, the child's method is found first. Polymorphism means that different objects can respond to the same method call in different ways, and method overriding is the primary mechanism for achieving this.

The instanceof operator checks whether an object's prototype chain includes a given constructor's prototype. It walks the chain from the object up to null, checking each __proto__ link against the constructor's prototype property. This is useful for type checking in runtime, but it has limitations — it does not work across different JavaScript realms (iframes, for example, have different global constructors).

JavaScript does not support multiple inheritance — a class can only extend one parent. Mixins offer a workaround. A mixin is typically a function that takes a base class and returns a new class extending it with additional behavior. By chaining mixin calls, you can compose multiple behaviors onto a single class without the diamond problem that plagues traditional multiple inheritance.

The composition vs inheritance debate is crucial in software design. Inheritance creates tight coupling — changes to the parent class ripple through all descendants. Composition, where objects contain instances of other objects and delegate behavior to them, creates looser coupling and more flexibility. The general guidance is to favor composition over inheritance. Use inheritance when there is a clear "is-a" relationship (a Dog is an Animal). Use composition when you want to combine behaviors (a Car has an Engine, has Wheels).

codeExamples.title: extends and super in constructors
codeExamples.output:
Rex says Woof
Rex fetches the ball
Rex
German Shepherd
[Animal: Rex]
codeExamples.explanation: Dog extends Animal, inheriting speak() and toString(). The super(name, 'Woof') call invokes Animal's constructor, setting name and sound. Dog adds its own breed property and fetch method.

codeExamples.title: Method overriding and super.method()
codeExamples.output:
Unknown with area 0.00
Rectangle with area 20.00 (4x5)
Square with area 9.00 (3x3)
[0, 20, 9]
codeExamples.explanation: Each class overrides area() to provide its own calculation. Rectangle.describe() calls super.describe() to include the parent's logic, then appends dimensions. The shapes array demonstrates polymorphism — the same method call produces different output depending on the actual object type.

codeExamples.title: instanceof and prototype chain checking
codeExamples.output:
true
true
true
true
true
false
codeExamples.explanation: instanceof walks the prototype chain. Since ElectricCar extends Car which extends Vehicle, tesla is an instance of all three. The myInstanceOf function demonstrates the underlying algorithm — it traverses __proto__ links looking for a match with the constructor's prototype.

codeExamples.title: Mixins and composition vs inheritance
codeExamples.output:
Hello, Alice
{"name":"Alice","email":"alice@example.com","createdAt":"2026-03-10"}
string
true
Engine started & Wheels rolling
codeExamples.explanation: Mixins compose behavior by wrapping base classes in higher-order functions. User inherits from BaseUser, Serializable, and Timestamped simultaneously. The ComposedCar example shows the alternative: instead of inheriting from Engine and Wheels, the car contains them and delegates. This is composition over inheritance.

notes: You must call super() in a subclass constructor before accessing this. Failing to do so is a ReferenceError.
Static methods are also inherited through extends — Child.staticMethod() works if Parent defines it.
JavaScript supports only single inheritance. Use mixins or composition to combine behaviors from multiple sources.
The extends keyword can be followed by any expression that resolves to a constructor — this is what enables the mixin pattern.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to call super() in a subclass constructor — this causes a ReferenceError when trying to use this.
Calling super() with the wrong arguments — the child must pass the arguments that the parent constructor expects.
Over-using inheritance when composition would be simpler — deep inheritance hierarchies become rigid and hard to modify.
Assuming instanceof works across iframes or different JavaScript realms — each realm has its own set of built-in constructors.

exercises.title: Shape Hierarchy with Polymorphism
exercises.description: Create a base class 'Shape' with a name and an area() method returning 0. Extend it with 'Circle' (takes radius, area = pi*r^2) and 'Triangle' (takes base and height, area = 0.5*b*h). Override area() in each subclass. Iterate over an array of shapes and call describe() to demonstrate polymorphism.
hints: Each subclass must call super(name) in its constructor before accessing 'this'. Pass the shape name string to the parent.
describe() is defined on the base class but calls this.area(), which is overridden in each subclass. This is polymorphism -- the same method call produces different results.

exercises.title: Method Override with super
exercises.description: Create a base class 'Logger' with a log(msg) method that returns the message prefixed with '[LOG]'. Create a child class 'TimestampLogger' that overrides log() to prepend a timestamp, calling super.log() to include the parent's prefix. Demonstrate the chain of overrides.
hints: super.log(msg) inside TimestampLogger calls the parent Logger's log method, returning '[LOG] Server started'. The child then wraps it with the timestamp.
instanceof checks the entire prototype chain, so a TimestampLogger instance is also an instance of Logger.

content.explanation:
Encapsulation is the principle of bundling data (properties) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (an object or class), while restricting direct access to some of the object's internals. The goal is to prevent external code from depending on or accidentally modifying internal implementation details. In JavaScript, encapsulation has evolved through multiple mechanisms: naming conventions, closures, symbols, WeakMaps, and most recently, true private fields with the # syntax.

The modern approach to encapsulation in JavaScript uses private fields and methods, declared with the # prefix. These are enforced at the language level — attempting to access a #field from outside the class body results in a SyntaxError at parse time, not a runtime error. This is a significant improvement over the underscore convention (_property), which is merely a signal to other developers. Public properties are the interface; private fields are the implementation.

Before ES2022 private fields existed, closures were the primary way to achieve true data hiding in JavaScript. By defining variables inside a constructor function's scope and exposing only specific methods (the closure) as properties, you could create objects with genuinely inaccessible internal state. This pattern is sometimes called the revealing module pattern when applied to single objects or IIFEs. While closure-based privacy works, it has a cost: every instance gets its own copy of the methods, unlike prototype methods which are shared.

Getters and setters provide controlled access to an object's internal state. Instead of exposing a raw property that anyone can read or write to, you define accessor methods that can validate inputs, compute derived values, log access, or trigger side effects. In classes, they are defined with the get and set keywords and are accessed like regular properties. This is a key part of encapsulation because it lets you change the internal representation without breaking external code that depends on the property.

Abstraction in JavaScript is about hiding complexity and exposing only what is necessary. JavaScript does not have a formal abstract keyword like Java or C#, but you can simulate abstract classes by throwing errors in base class methods that subclasses are expected to override. This pattern ensures that subclasses provide their own implementation. The combination of encapsulation (hide how it works) and abstraction (hide that it works at all) creates clean interfaces that are easy to use and hard to misuse.

A practical example of encapsulation and abstraction together is a user authentication class. External code calls authenticate(password) and gets back a boolean. Internally, the class hashes the password, compares hashes, manages attempt counters, and enforces lockout policies. None of these details leak out. If you later change from bcrypt to argon2, the external interface remains unchanged. This is the power of information hiding — implementation changes do not break consumers.

codeExamples.title: Private fields for encapsulation
codeExamples.explanation: The password hash, attempt counter, and lock state are all private. External code cannot read the password, reset attempts, or unlock the account — it can only interact through the authenticate method and isLocked getter. This is encapsulation in practice.

codeExamples.title: Closures for data hiding (pre-ES2022)
codeExamples.output:
11
12
11
[{ action: 'inc', value: 11 }, { action: 'inc', value: 12 }, { action: 'dec', value: 11 }]
11
undefined
undefined
codeExamples.explanation: The count and history variables live in the closure created by createCounter. The returned object's methods close over them, providing controlled access. Directly accessing counter.count returns undefined because it is not a property of the returned object — it is a local variable in the enclosing scope.

codeExamples.title: Getters and setters for controlled access
codeExamples.output:
999
Laptop: $799.20 (20% off)
Gaming Laptop: $1274.15 (15% off)
Price must be a non-negative number
codeExamples.explanation: Every property is guarded by setters that validate input. The finalPrice getter computes a derived value without storing it separately. If the internal pricing formula changes, external code continues to work because it only accesses finalPrice.

codeExamples.title: Abstract class pattern and information hiding
codeExamples.output:
DataStore is abstract — cannot instantiate directly
Saved 3 entries to MemoryStore
2
3
true
codeExamples.explanation: DataStore simulates an abstract class by throwing if instantiated directly (using new.target). Its save() and load() methods throw errors to force subclasses to provide implementations. The concrete saveAll() method works with any subclass that implements the abstract interface. MemoryStore hides its internal Map behind private fields.

notes: Private fields (#) are enforced by the engine at the syntax level, making them the strongest form of encapsulation in JavaScript.
Closures provide true privacy but at the cost of memory — each instance gets its own copy of methods, whereas prototype methods are shared.
The underscore prefix convention (_property) is not enforced — it is only a developer agreement that the property should not be accessed directly.
new.target inside a constructor tells you which constructor was actually called with new, enabling the abstract class pattern.
commonPitfalls: Relying on the underscore convention (_property) for real security — anyone can still access and modify _property freely.
Returning direct references to internal arrays or objects from getters — external code can mutate them. Always return copies with spread or structuredClone.
Making everything private — over-encapsulation makes classes hard to extend and test. Only hide true implementation details.
Forgetting that closure-based privacy means each instance gets its own method copies, increasing memory usage compared to prototype methods.

exercises.title: Closure-Based Secret Keeper
exercises.description: Use the closure pattern (no classes) to create a 'createVault' factory function that hides a secret string. The returned object should have methods to verify a guess and to get a hint (first and last character), but no way to access the secret directly.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Wrong!
Starts with 'c' and ends with 'e'
Correct!
undefined
hints: The 'secret' parameter is a local variable in createVault's scope. The returned methods close over it, but it is never exposed as a property.
vault.secret is undefined because 'secret' is not a property of the returned object -- it only exists in the closure scope.

exercises.title: Encapsulated Score Tracker
exercises.description: Create a class 'ScoreTracker' with a private #scores array. Add methods to record a score, get the average, and get the top score. The #scores array should never be directly accessible or modifiable from outside.
hints: Use Array.prototype.reduce to sum all scores for the average calculation. Divide by the array length.
Math.max(...this.#scores) spreads the array into individual arguments to find the highest score. The private field ensures no one can tamper with scores externally.
